original_abstract:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have garnered significant research interest, yet the field remains challenged by a limited understanding of their dynamic behavior under operational conditions. This lack of insight hinders both the rational design of catalysts and a deeper mechanical understanding of their performance. In this study, we investigate the evolution of active sites on a Pd/TiO₂-anatase SAC (Pd₁/TiO₂) during the reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) reaction. By integrating kinetic studies, in situ characterization, and theoretical modeling, we demonstrate that heating to temperatures ≥ 350 °C in H₂ reduces the TiO₂ support and modifies the coordination environment of the Pd. This process creates Pd sites characterized by partially cleaved interfacial Pd–O bonds and a unique electronic structure, which drive high intrinsic rWGS activity via a carboxyl pathway.

This H₂-induced activation occurs alongside the partial sintering of isolated Pd₁ atoms into disordered, flat clusters (Pdₙ) approximately 1 nm in diameter. While these highly active sites are eliminated by oxidation, high-temperature oxidative treatment successfully redisperses the Pdₙ clusters and facilitates subsequent TiO₂ reduction. Conversely, treatment with CO causes Pd₁ atoms to sinter into larger, crystalline 5 nm nanoparticles (Pd_NP), leading to catalyst deactivation. During the rWGS reaction, these two evolutionary pathways coexist; however, H₂ activation predominates, resulting in an increasing reaction rate over time and the formation of steady-state active sites similar to those generated under pure H₂. Our findings reveal how the coordination environment and nuclearity of metal sites on a SAC evolve during catalysis and pretreatment, illustrating how these dynamics modulate activity. These insights into the structure-function relationships of SACs are essential for the future design of more efficient and stable catalytic systems.
humanized_abstract:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have triggered a large amount of research, but their dynamic behavior remains poorly understood. This lack of understanding obstructs the development of catalysts with desired performance and obstructs the fundamental understanding of the mechanical mechanism of the catalysts. In this study, we investigated the state of active sites of a Pd/TiO₂-anatase single-atom catalyst (Pd₁/TiO₂) during reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) catalysis by integrating the kinetic studies, in situ synchrotron X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) measurements, and theoretical calculations. It is found that the Pd₁/TiO₂ catalytic activity towards rWGS was increased under H2 at temperatures ≥ 350 °C. Following the detailed reduction of the TiO₂ support and the changes in the Pd coordination environment at elevated temperatures in H2, the as-prepared Pd₁/TiO₂ catalyst exhibited highly active sites with partially broken Pd–O interfacial bonds and a distinct electronic configuration, which favours high intrinsic rWGS activity via a carboxyl route.

Hydrogen activation occurs together with the partial sintering of isolated Pd₁ species into disorder two-dimensional clusters denoted as Pdₙ, about 1 nm in diameter. Upon subsequent oxidation, the active species are lost. Upon heat treatment in air, Pdₙ is redispersed and this catalyst is ready for further TPR of TiO₂. Treatment in CO, instead, leads to the sintering of isolated Pd₁ species into 5 nm large 3D crystalline Pd_NP species with deactivation of the catalyst. Under rWGS, the two paths coexist. However H2 activation is prevailing and with increasing time on stream, catalytic activity is continuously increasing, until it remains at steady state value very close to that under continuous exposure to pure H2. Such study allowed elucidating how the coordination geometry and the metal atom coordination number of the metal species on SAC can evolve and affecting in turn the catalytic activity and, moreover, how the structure-function relationship on such kind of catalysts works, an essential information for a wise optimization of new catalytic materials with better catalytic performance and stability.

document.output:

document.input:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have garnered significant research interest, yet the field remains challenged by a limited understanding of their dynamic behavior under operational conditions. This lack of insight hinders both the rational design of catalysts and a deeper mechanical understanding of their performance. In this study, we investigate the evolution of active sites on a Pd/TiO₂-anatase SAC (Pd₁/TiO₂) during the reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) reaction. By integrating kinetic studies, in situ characterization, and theoretical modeling, we demonstrate that heating to temperatures ≥ 350 °C in H₂ reduces the TiO₂ support and modifies the coordination environment of the Pd. This process creates Pd sites characterized by partially cleaved interfacial Pd–O bonds and a unique electronic structure, which drive high intrinsic rWGS activity via a carboxyl pathway.

This H₂-induced activation occurs alongside the partial sintering of isolated Pd₁ atoms into disordered, flat clusters (Pdₙ) approximately 1 nm in diameter. While these highly active sites are eliminated by oxidation, high-temperature oxidative treatment successfully redisperses the Pdₙ clusters and facilitates subsequent TiO₂ reduction. Conversely, treatment with CO causes Pd₁ atoms to sinter into larger, crystalline 5 nm nanoparticles (Pd_NP), leading to catalyst deactivation. During the rWGS reaction, these two evolutionary pathways coexist; however, H₂ activation predominates, resulting in an increasing reaction rate over time and the formation of steady-state active sites similar to those generated under pure H₂. Our findings reveal how the coordination environment and nuclearity of metal sites on a SAC evolve during catalysis and pretreatment, illustrating how these dynamics modulate activity. These insights into the structure-function relationships of SACs are essential for the future design of more efficient and stable catalytic systems.
